text:
Awa, thou vaunting God o’ Day!
Awa, thou wan Diana!
Ilk Star, gang hide thy glittering ray,
When I am gaun to meet my Anna!
source_text:
Awa, thou flaunting God of Day!
Awa, thou pale Diana!
Ilk Star, gae hide thy twinkling ray,
When I’m to meet my Anna!

text:
Bards of Passion and of Mirth,  
Ye have left your souls on earth!  
Have ye souls in heaven too,  
Double-liv’d in realms anew?  
Ay, and those of heaven converse  
With the spheres of sun and moon;  
With the murmur of fountains wondrous,  
And the parley of voices thunderous;  
With the whisper of heaven’s trees  
And one another, in soft ease  
Seated on Elysian lawns  
Browsed by none but Dian’s fawns  
Beneath wide blue-bells canopied,  
Where the daisies breathe of rose,  
And the rose herself hath won  
Perfume unknown beneath the sun;  
Where the nightingale doth sing  
Not a void, enchanted thing,  
But divine melodious truth;  
Philosophic numbers smooth;  
Tales and golden histories  
Of heaven and its mysteries.
source_text:
Bards of Passion and of Mirth,
Ye have left your souls on earth!
Have ye souls in heaven too,
Double-lived in regions new?
Yes, and those of heaven commune
With the spheres of sun and moon;
With the noise of fountains wond'rous,
And the parle of voices thund'rous;
With the whisper of heaven's trees
And one another, in soft ease
Seated on Elysian lawns
Brows'd by none but Dian's fawns
Underneath large blue-bells tented,
Where the daisies are rose-scented,
And the rose herself has got
Perfume which on earth is not;
Where the nightingale doth sing
Not a senseless, tranced thing,
But divine melodious truth;
Philosophic numbers smooth;
Tales and golden histories
Of heaven and its mysteries.

text:
Her father answered, with a cheerful steadfastness of belief he could scarce have feigned, “Quite sure, my darling! Yea more than that,”  
he added, as he kissed her tenderly: “my future is far more radiant, Lucie, when beheld through thy marriage, than it could have been—  
nay, than it ever was—without it.”
source_text: Her father answered, with a cheerful firmness of conviction he could scarcely have assumed, “Quite sure, my darling! More than that,” he added, as he tenderly kissed her: “my future is far brighter, Lucie, seen through your marriage, than it could have been--nay, than it ever was--without it.”

text:
Poet:
Fresh and rose-hued red the sun is climbing high,
There floats the sea in far-off blue, careering through its channels,
There floats the wind above the bosom of the sea, setting landward,
The great steadfast wind from west or west by south,
Floating so buoyant, with milk-white foam upon the waters.
source_text:
Poet:
Fresh and rosy red the sun is mounting high,
On floats the sea in distant blue careering through its channels,
On floats the wind over the breast of the sea setting in toward land,
The great steady wind from west or west-by-south,
Floating so buoyant with milk-white foam on the waters.

text:
By my guid fortune I did meet  
A maiden, in the midst of care,  
And kindly she did bid me go  
To walk into a chamber fair.
source_text:
By my gude luck a maid I met,
Just in the middle o’ my care,
And kindly she did me invite
To walk into a chamber fair.

text:
Time is a trial of sorrow,
Yet not a cure at all.
If such it should but so appear,
Then no ill did befall.
source_text:
Time is a test of trouble,
But not a remedy.
If such it prove, it prove too
There was no malady.

text:
It’s scarce within a body’s pow’r
At times to keep frae turning sour,
To mark how things are shar’d;
How best o’ chiels are whiles in want,
While coofs on heaps o’ thousands rant,
And kenna how to wair’t;
But, Davie, lad, ne’er fash your head,
Tho’ we hae little gear;
We’re fit to win our daily bread,
As lang’s we’re hale and fier:
“Mair spier na, nor fear na,”
Auld age ne’er minds a feg;
The last o’t, the warst o’t
Is only but to beg.
source_text:
It’s hardly in a body’s pow’r
To keep, at times, frae being sour,
To see how things are shar’d;
How best o’ chiels are whiles in want,
While coofs on countless thousands rant,
And ken na how to wair’t;
But, Davie, lad, ne’er fash your head,
Tho’ we hae little gear;
We’re fit to win our daily bread,
As lang’s we’re hale and fier:
“Mair spier na, nor fear na,”
Auld age ne’er mind a feg;
The last o’t, the warst o’t
Is only but to beg.

text:
With piteous gaze she on dead, senseless things  
Would look, her lost Basil amorously seeking;  
And, with a tuneful quaver in the strings  
Of her forlorn voice, oft she fell to speaking,  
Calling after the Pilgrim in his wanderings,  
To ask him where her Basil lay, and, shrinking,  
Why it from her was hid: “For cruel ’tis,” quoth she,  
“To bear away my Basil-pot from me.”
source_text:
Piteous she look'd on dead and senseless things,
Asking for her lost Basil amorously;
And with melodious chuckle in the strings
Of her lorn voice, she oftentimes would cry
After the Pilgrim in his wanderings,
To ask him where her Basil was; and why
'Twas hid from her: "For cruel 'tis," said she,
"To steal my Basil-pot away from me."

text:
I know what was, I feel full well what is,  
And I should rave, if spirits could go mad;  
Though I forget the savour of earth’s bliss,  
That pallor warms my grave, as though I had  
A Seraph chosen from the bright abyss  
To be my spouse: thy pallor makes me glad;  
Thy beauty grows upon me, and I feel  
A greater love through all my being steal.
source_text:
"I know what was, I feel full well what is,
And I should rage, if spirits could go mad;
Though I forget the taste of earthly bliss,
That paleness warms my grave, as though I had
A Seraph chosen from the bright abyss
To be my spouse: thy paleness makes me glad;
Thy beauty grows upon me, and I feel
A greater love through all my essence steal."

text:
I have heard it in the coldest land,  
And upon the most unearthly sea;  
Yet never, in my sorest need,  
Did it beg a crumb of me.
source_text:
I 've heard it in the chillest land,
And on the strangest sea;
Yet, never, in extremity,
It asked a crumb of me.

text:
‘And, father, how may I love thee
Or any brother dearer still?
I love thee like the little bird
That pecks for crumbs beside the sill.’
source_text:
‘And, father, how can I love you
Or any of my brothers more?
I love you like the little bird
That picks up crumbs around the door.’

text:
This is a face of bitter herbs, this a draught that turns the stomach, they need no naming,
And more from the apothecary’s shelf, laudanum, caoutchouc, or hog’s-lard.
source_text:
This is a face of bitter herbs, this an emetic, they need no label,
And more of the drug-shelf, laudanum, caoutchouc, or hog’s-lard.

text:
I rose betimes, and led my dog,
And wandered down to sea;
The mermaids in the cellar-dim
Came forth to gaze on me,
source_text:
I started early, took my dog,
And visited the sea;
The mermaids in the basement
Came out to look at me,

text:
Along the margin-sand huge foot-prints ran,
No farther than the place whereon his feet had stray'd,
And there had slept ever since. Upon the soaked ground
His old right hand lay nerveless, listless, dead,
Unsceptred; and his realm-less eyes were closed;
While his bow'd head seem'd hearkening to the Earth,
His ancient mother, for some comfort yet.
source_text:
Along the margin-sand large foot-marks went,
No further than to where his feet had stray'd,
And slept there since. Upon the sodden ground
His old right hand lay nerveless, listless, dead,
Unsceptred; and his realmless eyes were closed;
While his bow'd head seem'd list'ning to the Earth,
His ancient mother, for some comfort yet.

text:
“I would not alter it. How rich with joy
Is this blessed day! Ah, my Annis, thou shalt soon
Be join’d unto thy sister—thou’rt upon thy way,
And these are merciful friends who will not hinder.”
source_text: “I would not change it. How rich is this day in happiness! Ah, my Annis, thou shalt join thy sister soon--thou'rt on thy way, and these be merciful friends that will not hinder.”

text:
Without a blush the man I like knows and avows the sweetness of his sex,
Without a blush the woman I like knows and avows hers.
source_text:
Without shame the man I like knows and avows the deliciousness of his sex,
Without shame the woman I like knows and avows hers.

text:
How vain  
Are words! I deemed I ne'er should speak again,  
Not even in secret,—not to mine own heart—  
Yet from my lips the loathful accents part,  
And from my pen the phrases stream as I write,  
Blinding mine eyes with scalding tears... my sight  
Grows dim to view that traced in vain  
Upon this senseless leaf which burns the brain  
And gnaws into it... blotting all things fair  
And wise and good which time had written there.
source_text:
‘How vain
Are words! I thought never to speak again,
Not even in secret,—not to mine own heart—
But from my lips the unwilling accents start,
And from my pen the words flow as I write,
Dazzling my eyes with scalding tears...my sight
Is dim to see that charactered in vain
On this unfeeling leaf which burns the brain
And eats into it...blotting all things fair
And wise and good which time had written there.

text:
But, in love’s common award,
The small delight doth quell much bitterness;
Though Dido silent lies in the under-grove,
And Isabella’s was a mighty distress,
Though young Lorenzo in the warm Indian clove
Was not embalm’d, this truth is not the less--
Even bees, the little almsmen of spring-bowers,
Know there is richest juice in poison-flowers.
source_text:
But, for the general award of love,
The little sweet doth kill much bitterness;
Though Dido silent is in under-grove,
And Isabella's was a great distress,
Though young Lorenzo in warm Indian clove
Was not embalm'd, this truth is not the less--
Even bees, the little almsmen of spring-bowers,
Know there is richest juice in poison-flowers.

text:
Chorus.—Theniel Menzies’ bonnie Mary,
Theniel Menzies’ bonnie Mary,
Charlie Grigor lost his plaidie,
Kissin’ Theniel’s bonnie Mary.
source_text:
Chorus.—Theniel Menzies’ bonie Mary,
Theniel Menzies’ bonie Mary,
Charlie Grigor tint his plaidie,
Kissin’ Theniel’s bonie Mary.

text:
The Hero of these artless lays,  
A humble bard was he,  
Who sang his rhymes in Coila’s plains,  
With meikle mirth an’ glee;  
Kind Nature’s care had dealt his share  
Full large of the flaming current;  
And, all devout, he never sought  
To stem the sacred torrent.
source_text:
The Hero of these artless strains,
A lowly bard was he,
Who sung his rhymes in Coila’s plains,
With meikle mirth an’glee;
Kind Nature’s care had given his share
Large, of the flaming current;
And, all devout, he never sought
To stem the sacred torrent.

text:
’Tis to behold your children frail  
With their mothers pine and pale,  
When the winter winds blow chill and keen,—  
They are dying while I speak.
source_text:
‘’Tis to see your children weak
With their mothers pine and peak,
When the winter winds are bleak,—
They are dying whilst I speak.

text: “Thou forget’st that Launcelot abides herein; and where Launcelot doth dwell, she marks not the king’s departing, nor the day upon which he returneth.”
source_text: “Thou forgettest that Launcelot is here; and where Launcelot is, she noteth not the going forth of the king, nor what day he returneth.”

text:
Year after year,
Through blood and tears,
And a deep hell of hatreds, and hopes, and fears;
We waded and flew,
And the isles were but few
Where the bud-blighted blossoms of happiness grew.
source_text:
Years after years,
Through blood, and tears,
And a thick hell of hatreds, and hopes, and fears;
We waded and flew,
And the islets were few
Where the bud-blighted flowers of happiness grew.

text:
And, furthermore, her brethren much did muse
Why she sat drooping by the Basil green,
And wherefore it so flourish'd, as with use
Of some enchantment; sorely were they seen
To wonder what the thing might signify:
They could not verily believe that such
A very nothing could have power to draw
Her from her own fair youth, and pleasures gay,
And even remembrance of her love's delay.
source_text:
And, furthermore, her brethren wonder'd much
Why she sat drooping by the Basil green,
And why it flourish'd, as by magic touch;
Greatly they wonder'd what the thing might mean:
They could not surely give belief, that such
A very nothing would have power to wean
Her from her own fair youth, and pleasures gay,
And even remembrance of her love's delay.

text:
Why, what have thou deemed of thyself?  
Is it thou, then, that held thyself less?  
Is it thou that deemed the President mightier than thou?  
Or the wealthy more blest than thou? or the learned wiser than thou?
source_text:
Why what have you thought of yourself?
Is it you then that thought yourself less?
Is it you that thought the President greater than you?
Or the rich better off than you? or the educated wiser than you?

text:
Now nearer sound the bugles,  
And the drums beat more convulsively,  
And the daylight o’er the pavement quite hath faded,  
And the stern dead-march wrapt me round.
source_text:
Now nearer blow the bugles,
And the drums strike more convulsive,
And the daylight o’er the pavement quite has faded,
And the strong dead-march enwraps me.

text:
A spider stitched by night  
Without a light  
Across a curve of white.  
Whether it was a lady’s ruff  
Or a gnome’s own shroud,  
He kept that knowledge tucked.  
Of immortality  
His policy  
Was face and form to see.
source_text:
A spider sewed at night
Without a light
Upon an arc of white.
If ruff it was of dame
Or shroud of gnome,
Himself, himself inform.
Of immortality
His strategy
Was physiognomy.

text:
You let a purple ravel fall,
You let an amber strand;
And now you’ve strewn the whole East
With scraps of emerald!
source_text:
You dropped a purple ravelling in,
You dropped an amber thread;
And now you 've littered all the East
With duds of emerald!

text:
See, see old Orthodoxy’s foes  
She’s thrashin’ through the city!  
Hark, how the nine-tailed cat she throws!  
I swear it’s awfu’ pretty:  
There Learning, wi’ his Greekish face,  
Growls out a Latin ditty;  
And Common-sense is gaun, she says,  
To make to Jamie Beattie her plaint this day.
source_text:
See, see auld Orthodoxy’s faes
She’s swingein thro’ the city!
Hark, how the nine-tail’d cat she plays!
I vow it’s unco pretty:
There, Learning, with his Greekish face,
Grunts out some Latin ditty;
And Common-sense is gaun, she says,
To mak to Jamie Beattie
Her plaint this day.

[Published in "A Masque of Poets"
at the request of "H.H.," the author's

source_text:

text:
They were the pride of the ranger line,
Peerless in saddle, rifle, song, supper, and wooing,
Great-hearted, restless, generous, comely, lofty, and tender,
Bearded, weathered, clad in the loose garb of hunters,
Not one among them past thirty years.
source_text:
They were the glory of the race of rangers,
Matchless with horse, rifle, song, supper, courtship,
Large, turbulent, generous, handsome, proud, and affectionate,
Bearded, sunburnt, drest in the free costume of hunters,
Not a single one over thirty years of age.

text:
Let the thundering guns go by  
Till the still air seems to fly  
With the clang of grinding wheels,  
And the beat of horses’ heels.
source_text:
‘Let the charged artillery drive
Till the dead air seems alive
With the clash of clanging wheels,
And the tramp of horses’ heels.

text:
Hey, the dusty miller,
And his dusty sack;
Bless me for the calling
That fills the dusty peck:
Fills the dusty peck,
Brings the dusty silver;
I would give my coat
For the dusty miller.
source_text:
Hey, the dusty Miller,
And his dusty sack;
Leeze me on the calling
Fills the dusty peck:
Fills the dusty peck,
Brings the dusty siller;
I wad gie my coatie
For the dusty Miller.

text:
Come on! the temptations shall be richer,
We will cross uncharted and wild seas,
We will go where the winds blow, the waves break, and the Yankee clipper
sweeps by with every sail spread full.
source_text:
Allons! the inducements shall be greater,
We will sail pathless and wild seas,
We will go where winds blow, waves dash, and the Yankee clipper
speeds by under full sail.

text:
The hero of these simple lays,  
A humble bard was he,  
Who sang his rhymes on Coila’s braes  
With muckle mirth and glee;  
Kind Nature’s hand had given his part  
A liberal flame and flow;  
And, reverent still, he never strove  
That hallowed tide to stay.
source_text:
The Hero of these artless strains,
A lowly bard was he,
Who sung his rhymes in Coila’s plains,
With meikle mirth an’glee;
Kind Nature’s care had given his share
Large, of the flaming current;
And, all devout, he never sought
To stem the sacred torrent.

text:
For all his meat and all his ale,
For all his fresh beef, salt and hale,
For all his gold and silver free,
An auld man never shall daunt me.
To daunt me, &c.
source_text:
For a’ his meal and a’ his maut,
For a’ his fresh beef and his saut,
For a’ his gold and white monie,
And auld men shall never daunton me.
To daunton me, &c.

text:
ORSINO:
Why, what need is here?
Who ever feared the pale approach of remorse
In a just deed? Though our first design has failed,
Doubt not, he will soon be laid to rest.
But light the lamp; let us not speak in the dark.
source_text:
ORSINO:
Why, what need of this?
Who feared the pale intrusion of remorse
In a just deed? Although our first plan failed,
Doubt not but he will soon be laid to rest.
But light the lamp; let us not talk i’ the dark.

text:
Of one calm and unattained star,
As though it were a lamp of mortal light,
Unknowing, as are certain human lovers,
source_text:
Of one serene and unapproached star,
As if it were a lamp of earthly light,
Unconscious, as some human lovers are,

text:
Him all wait for, to him all surrender, his word is final and decisive,  
Him they receive, in him bathe, in him feel themselves as though in light,  
Him they sink into and he sinks into them.
source_text:
Him all wait for, him all yield up to, his word is decisive and final,
Him they accept, in him lave, in him perceive themselves as amid light,
Him they immerse and he immerses them.

text:
Her little form beside the gate  
The angels must have seen,  
For I could never find her  
On this frail, earthly scene.
source_text:
Her little figure at the gate
The angels must have spied,
Since I could never find her
Upon the mortal side.

text:
O Gowdie, fear o’ the whigs,  
Dread o’ blackcoats and reverend wigs!  
Sour Bigotry, with failing legs,  
Grins and turns back,  
Wishing the ten Egyptian plagues  
Might seize you quick.
source_text:
O Gowdie, terror o’ the whigs,
Dread o’ blackcoats and rev’rend wigs!
Sour Bigotry, on her last legs,
Girns an’ looks back,
Wishing the ten Egyptian plagues
May seize you quick.

text:
I honor Assyria, China, Teutonia, and the Hebrews,
I take up every doctrine, myth, god, and demigod,
I find the old narratives, bibles, genealogies, true, without
exception,
I declare that all days of the past were what they had to be,
And that there was no way they could have been better than they were,
And that today is what it must be, and that America is,
And that today and America could no-how be better than they are.
source_text:
I respect Assyria, China, Teutonia, and the Hebrews,
I adopt each theory, myth, god, and demigod,
I see that the old accounts, bibles, genealogies, are true, without
exception,
I assert that all past days were what they must have been,
And that they could no-how have been better than they were,
And that to-day is what it must be, and that America is,
And that to-day and America could no-how be better than they are.

text:
Sun’s proud display, I need not your warm lie—move on!
You touch only the face of things; I drive through the skin and the depths as well.
source_text:
Flaunt of the sunshine I need not your bask--lie over!
You light surfaces only, I force surfaces and depths also.

text:
Of robins in the trundle bed  
How many I could spy  
Whose nightclothes could not mask their wings,  
Though I could hear them try!
source_text:
Of robins in the trundle bed
How many I espy
Whose nightgowns could not hide the wings,
Although I heard them try!

text:
She, all those human shapes that breathed before her,  
Beheld as living spirits—to her eyes  
The soul’s unclothed beauty stood laid open there,  
And often through a coarse and timeworn guise  
She saw the inward form most radiant, fair—  
And then she held a charm of curious guise,  
Which, breathed upon shut lips in tender tone,  
Could make that spirit mingle with her own.
source_text:
She, all those human figures breathing there,
Beheld as living spirits—to her eyes
The naked beauty of the soul lay bare,
And often through a rude and worn disguise
She saw the inner form most bright and fair—
And then she had a charm of strange device,
Which, murmured on mute lips with tender tone,
Could make that spirit mingle with her own.

text:
O, you give true warning indeed, you bell among the sea-reefs tolling,  
Tolling, tolling, to keep the ship from the place of wreck.
source_text:
O you give good notice indeed, you bell by the sea-reefs ringing,
Ringing, ringing, to warn the ship from its wreck-place.

text:
Father:
Hold, hold, my silly child,
What you are uttering grieves me sorely, and much discontents me;
Look with the others once again, I say, look not on banners and pennons high,
But look on the laid and finished pavements, and note the steadfast-walled houses.
source_text:
Father:
Cease, cease, my foolish babe,
What you are saying is sorrowful to me, much ’t displeases me;
Behold with the rest again I say, behold not banners and pennants aloft,
But the well-prepared pavements behold, and mark the solid-wall’d houses.

text:
A shudder passed through the Warrior’s frame,
More chill than the nocturnal wind,
More chill than dew at evening’s end,
When twilight’s hour had slipped behind.—
source_text:
A shivering through the Warrior flew,
Colder than the nightly blast,
Colder than the evening dew,
When the hour of twilight’s past.—

text:
Her audience is the noon,  
Her providence the sun,  
Her progress by the bee made known  
In sovereign, unwavering tune.
source_text:
Her public is the noon,
Her providence the sun,
Her progress by the bee proclaimed
In sovereign, swerveless tune.

text:
I had no time to hate, because  
The grave would stay my hand,  
And life was not so spacious I  
Could bring my enmity to end.
source_text:
I had no time to hate, because
The grave would hinder me,
And life was not so ample I
Could finish enmity.

text:
Joy visited the Spirit,—  
Such joy as when a lover sees  
The one his soul has chosen in delight,  
And witnesses her peace  
Whose sorrow to him were keener than death,  
Sees her unwaning cheek  
Glow, flushed with the first sweet plenty of health,  
Thrilled by her lovely eyes,  
Which like two stars amid the heaving main  
Gleam through a tide of liquid bliss.
source_text:
Joy to the Spirit came,—
Such joy as when a lover sees
The chosen of his soul in happiness,
And witnesses her peace
Whose woe to him were bitterer than death,
Sees her unfaded cheek
Glow mantling in first luxury of health,
Thrills with her lovely eyes,
Which like two stars amid the heaving main
Sparkle through liquid bliss.

text:
I hear and see God in every thing, yet know God not at all,  
Nor do I know who could be more wondrous than I am.
source_text:
I hear and behold God in every object, yet understand God not in the least,
Nor do I understand who there can be more wonderful than myself.

text:
The dust renewed on lifted roads,
The birds with merrier voices sang;
The sunshine cast his hat aside,
The orchards’ spangles swung.
source_text:
The dust replaced in hoisted roads,
The birds jocoser sung;
The sunshine threw his hat away,
The orchards spangles hung.

text:
Now I know they both came by
And made their home in the wild,
Yet it was only because they died, — a truth
Kept back from the little child!
source_text:
Now know I they both visited
And settled regions wild,
But did because they died, -- a fact
Withheld the little child!

text:
SECOND FURY:
The sweetness of delight makes lovers glad,
Gazing on one another: so are we.
As from the rose the pale priestess kneels
To gather for her festal crown of flowers,
The airy crimson falls, and flushes her cheek,
So from our victim’s destined agony
The shadow that becomes our form invests us round,
Else we are shapeless as our mother Night.
source_text:
SECOND FURY:
The beauty of delight makes lovers glad,
Gazing on one another: so are we.
As from the rose which the pale priestess kneels
To gather for her festal crown of flowers
The aereal crimson falls, flushing her cheek,
So from our victim’s destined agony
The shade which is our form invests us round,
Else we are shapeless as our mother Night.

text:
l. 226. thyrsus. A rod wreathed in ivy, its head a fir-cone,
borne by Bacchus and those who follow him.
source_text:
l. 226. thyrsus. A rod wreathed with ivy and crowned with a fir-cone,
used by Bacchus and his followers.

text:
Victory comes too late,  
And is held close to lips gone numb  
Too spellbound by the frost  
To drink it.  
How sweet it might have been,  
Only one drop!  
Was God so frugal?  
His table is set too high for us  
Unless we sup on tiptoe.  
Crumbs suit such tiny mouths,  
Cherries suit the robins;  
The eagle’s golden meal  
Chokes them.  
God keeps his promise with sparrows,  
Who, with little love,  
Know how to hunger!
source_text:
Victory comes late,
And is held low to freezing lips
Too rapt with frost
To take it.
How sweet it would have tasted,
Just a drop!
Was God so economical?
His table 's spread too high for us
Unless we dine on tip-toe.
Crumbs fit such little mouths,
Cherries suit robins;
The eagle's golden breakfast
Strangles them.
God keeps his oath to sparrows,
Who of little love
Know how to starve!

text: Prior global models assign labels based on the original textual information and enhance the decoder using predefined hierarchical paths. By contrast, we propose a new end-to-end hierarchy-aware global model (HiAGM) designed to enable mutual interaction between text features and label correlations. The model integrates a conventional text classification component with a hierarchy encoder, thereby producing label-specific text features. HiAGM is further developed into two variants: a parallel model for inductive fusion (HiAGM-LA) and a serial model for deductive fusion (HiAGM-TP).
source_text: Previous global models classify labels upon the original textual information and improve the decoder with predefined hierarchy paths. In contrast, we construct a novel end-to-end hierarchy-aware global model (HiAGM) for the mutual interaction of text features and label correlations. It combines a traditional text classification model with a hierarchy encoder, thus obtaining label-wise text features. HiAGM is extended to two variants, a parallel model for an inductive fusion (HiAGM-LA) and a serial model for a deductive fusion (HiAGM-TP).

text: SEQ2SEQ models are highly effective for generation tasks. However, the 2,028 labeled question–response pairs in our SG train set (Table 1) are insufficient for training these large neural models. Conversely, constructing a new large-scale dataset that enables fluent answer generation through crowdsourcing is both inefficient and costly. We therefore augment SQuAD 2.0 with responses from the STs+BERT classifier (Table 2) to build a synthetic training dataset for SEQ2SEQ models. Specifically, we use all QA pairs from the SQuAD 2.0 training set that can be processed by the question parser and STs, and rank their candidate responses by the probabilities produced by the BERT response classifier trained with Softmax loss (i.e. ranking loss (Collins and Koo, 2005)). Thus, for each question, we select the top-ranked responses 7 by applying a threshold to the probabilities returned by the BERT model. We call the resulting dataset SQuAD-Synthetic (SS), which contains 59,738 q, a, r instances.
source_text: SEQ2SEQ models are very effective in generation tasks. However, our 2028 labeled question and response pairs from the SG train set (Table 1) are insufficient for training these large neural models. On the other hand, creating a new large-scale dataset that supports fluent answer generation by crowdsourcing is inefficient and expensive. Therefore, we augment SQuAD 2.0 with responses from the STs+BERT classifier (Table 2) to create a synthetic training dataset for SEQ2SEQ models. We take all the QA pairs from the SQuAD 2.0 train-set which can be handled by the question parser and STs, and rank their candidate responses using the BERT response classifier probabilities trained with Softmax loss (i.e. ranking loss (Collins and Koo, 2005) ). Therefore, for each question we select the top ranked responses 7 by setting a threshold on the probabilities obtained from the BERT model. We refer to the resulting dataset as SQuAD-Synthetic (SS) consisting of 59,738 q, a, r instances.

text: Automatic Detection The increasing prevalence of machine-generated content has spurred the development of automated methods for identifying it. GROVER was created not only to produce plausible news passages, but also to detect them through a fine-tuned variant of the generative model itself (Zellers et al., 2019). GLTR, which assumes that attackers will rely on sampling techniques that prefer high-probability tokens, seeks to expose machine-generated text by constructing histograms over per-token log-likelihoods (Gehrmann et al., 2019). Bakhtin et al. (2019) treat human-text detection as a ranking problem and assess cross-domain and cross-model generalization, observing a substantial drop in performance when models are trained on one domain and tested on another. Schuster et al. (2019) contend that the language distributional properties implicitly or explicitly used by such detectors are inadequate; instead, they propose turning to explicit fact-verification models. Finally, discriminators for distinguishing machine-generated text represent a promising avenue in adversarial training (Lin et al., 2017) and in the automatic evaluation of generative model quality (Novikova et al., 2017; Kannan and Vinyals, 2017; Lowe et al., 2017).
source_text: Automatic Detection The rise of machinegenerated content has led to the development of automated systems to identify it. GROVER was designed to not only generate convincing news excerpts but to also identify them using a fine-tuned version of the generative model itself (Zellers et al., 2019) . GLTR, expecting attackers to use sampling methods that favor high-likelihood tokens, aims to make machine-generated text detectable by computing histograms over per-token log likelihoods (Gehrmann et al., 2019) . Bakhtin et al. (2019) frame human-text detection as a ranking task and evaluate their models' cross-domain and cross-model generalization, finding significant loss in quality when training on one domain and evaluating on another. Schuster et al. (2019) argue that the language distributional features implicitly or explicitly employed by these detectors are insufficient; instead, one should look to explicit fact-verification models. Finally, discriminators for whether text is machine-generated are a promising research direction in adversarial training (Lin et al., 2017; and in automatic evaluation of generative model quality (Novikova et al., 2017; Kannan and Vinyals, 2017; Lowe et al., 2017) .

text: In contrast to our approach, which tests novel words in familiar combinations, many of these studies examine systematicity by evaluating familiar words in novel combinations. Lake and Baroni (2018) adopt this strategy in semantic parsing using an artificial language called SCAN. Dasgupta et al. (2018, 2019) introduce a naturalistic NLI dataset whose test instances permute the argument structure of natural-language utterances. In the inductive logic programming setting, Sinha et al. (2019) proposed the CLUTTR relational-reasoning benchmark. The familiar-words-in-novel-combinations paradigm was formalized by Keysers et al. (2019) in the CFQ dataset and its corresponding distribution metric. Ettinger et al. (2018) introduced a semantic-role-labeling and negation-scope-labeling dataset that evaluates compositional generalization through novel combinations of familiar words and incorporates syntactic constructions such as relative clauses. Finally, Kim et al. (2019) investigate pre-training schemes’ capacity to learn prepositions and wh-words under syntactic transformations, two types of closed-class words that our work does not address.
source_text: In contrast to our approach (testing novel words in familiar combinations), many of these studies probe systematicity by testing familiar words in novel combinations. Lake and Baroni (2018) adopt this approach in semantic parsing with an artificial language known as SCAN. Dasgupta et al. (2018 Dasgupta et al. ( , 2019 introduce a naturalistic NLI dataset, with test items that shuffle the argument structure of natural language utterances. In the in the inductive logic programming domain, Sinha et al. (2019) introduced the CLUTTR relational-reasoning benchmark. The novel-combinations-of-familiar-words approach was formalized in the CFQ dataset and associated distribution metric of Keysers et al. (2019) . Ettinger et al. (2018) introduced a semantic-rolelabeling and negation-scope labeling dataset, which tests compositional generalization with novel combinations of familiar words and makes use of syntactic constructions like relative clauses. Finally, Kim et al. (2019) explore pre-training schemes' abilities to learn prepositions and wh-words with syntactic transformations (two kinds of closedclass words which our work does not address).

text: It has been demonstrated that multi-task learning (MTL; Caruana, 1997; Liu et al., 2015, 2019b) exerts a regularizing effect by mitigating overfitting to a particular task. An open question is whether MTL also benefits SMART, and this section addresses that question. Following Liu et al. (2019b), we first “pre-trained” shared embeddings with MTL together with SMART, referred to as MT-DNN-SMART 8, and then adapted the training data for each task on top of the shared embeddings. We also include a baseline that fine-tuned each task. We observe that both MT-DNN and SMART consistently outperform the BERT model on all five GLUE tasks. Moreover, SMART achieves better results than MT-DNN on MNLI, QNLI, and MRPC, whereas it performs worse on RTE and SST, indicating that MT-DNN is a strong complement to SMART. By combining these two models, MT-DNN-SMART v0 inherits the advantages of both and thus improves the final results. For instance, it attains 85.7% (+0.1%) on MNLI and 80.2% (+1.1%) on RTE compared with the best results of MT-DNN and SMART, demonstrating that these two techniques are orthogonal. Finally, we also trained SMART jointly and then fine-tuned it on each task, as in Liu et al. (2019b). We observe that MT-DNN-SMART outperforms MT-DNN-SMART v0 and MT-DNN across all five tasks, except for MT-DNN on SST, showing that SMART improves the generalization of MTL.
source_text: It has been shown that multi-task learning (MTL, Caruana (1997) ; Liu et al. (2015 Liu et al. ( , 2019b ) has a regularization effect via alleviating overfitting to a specific task. One question is whether MTL helps SMART as well. In this section, we are going to answer this question. Following Liu et al. (2019b), we first "pre-trained" shared embeddings using MTL with SMART, denoted as MT-DNN-SMART 8 , and then adapted the training data on each task on top of the shared embeddings. We also include a baseline which fine-tuned each task We observe that both MT-DNN and SMART consistently outperform the BERT model on all five GLUE tasks. Furthermore, SMART outperforms MT-DNN on MNLI, QNLI, and MRPC, while it obtains worse results on RTE and SST, showing that MT-DNN is a strong counterpart for SMART. By combining these two models, MT-DNN-SMART v0 enjoys advantages of both and thus improved the final results. For example, it achieves 85.7% (+0.1%) on MNLI and 80.2% (+1.1%) on RTE comparing with the best results of MT-DNN and SMART demonstrating that these two techniques are orthogonal. Lastly we also trained SMART jointly and then finetuned on each task like Liu et al. (2019b) . We observe that MT-DNN-SMART outperformes MT-DNN-SMART v0 and MT-DNN across all 5 tasks (except MT-DNN on SST) showing that SMART improves the generalization of MTL.

text: In this work, we begin from a principled formulation of relational triple extraction at the level of triples themselves. This leads to a general algorithmic framework that is inherently able to address the overlapping triple problem. At the heart of the framework lies a novel viewpoint: rather than treating relations as discrete labels assigned to entity pairs, we model relations as functions that map subjects to objects. More specifically, instead of training relation classifiers f (s, o) → r, we learn relation-specific taggers f r (s) → o, each of which identifies the possible object(s) for a given subject under a particular relation, or yields no object, indicating that no triple exists for the given subject-relation pair. Within this framework, triple extraction proceeds in two stages: first, all possible subjects in a sentence are identified; then, for each subject, relation-specific taggers are applied to jointly detect all possible relations and their corresponding objects.
source_text: In this work, we start with a principled formulation of relational triple extraction right at the triple level. This gives rise to a general algorithmic framework that handles the overlapping triple problem by design. At the core of the framework is the fresh perspective that instead of treating relations as discrete labels on entity pairs, we can model relations as functions that map subjects to objects. More precisely, instead of learning relation classifiers f (s, o) → r, we learn relation-specific taggers f r (s) → o, each of which recognizes the possible object(s) of a given subject under a specific relation; or returns no object, indicating that there is no triple with the given subject and relation. Under this framework, triple extraction is a two-step process: first we identify all possible subjects in a sentence; then for each subject, we apply relationspecific taggers to simultaneously identify all possible relations and the corresponding objects.

text: Human Evaluation We additionally conducted two judgment-elicitation studies on the Amazon Mechanical Turk (AMT) crowdsourcing platform. The first study evaluated summary quality with Best-Worst Scaling (BWS; Louviere et al., 2015), a less labor-intensive alternative to pairwise comparisons that has been shown to yield more reliable results than rating scales (Kiritchenko and Mohammad, 2017). In particular, participants were shown the movie/business name, some basic background information, and a gold-standard summary. They also saw three system-generated summaries produced by SENTINEURON (the best extractive model), MEANSUM (the most related unsupervised model), and DENOISESUM.
source_text: Human Evaluation We also conducted two judgment elicitation studies using the Amazon Mechanical Turk (AMT) crowdsourcing platform. The first study assessed the quality of the summaries using Best-Worst Scaling (BWS; Louviere et al., 2015) , a less labor-intensive alternative to paired comparisons that has been shown to produce more reliable results than rating scales (Kiritchenko and Mohammad, 2017) . Specifically, participants were shown the movie/business name, some basic background information, and a gold-standard summary. They were also presented with three system summaries, produced by SENTINEURON (best extractive model), MEANSUM (most related unsupervised model), and DENOISESUM.

text: The first section of Table 2 reports the results for sentence-level multimodal QE using BiRNN. The strongest model is BiRNN+Vis-embed-mult2, which attains a Pearson’s r of 0.535 and significantly exceeds the baseline (p-value < 0.01). It can therefore be concluded that visual features are able to substantially improve the performance of sentence-level neural-based QE systems. Figure 2 shows the outcome of the Williams significance test for the BiRNN model variants. The figure is presented as a correlation matrix, which should be interpreted as follows: the value in cell (i, j) corresponds to the p-value of the Williams test for the performance difference between the model in row i and the model in column j (Graham, 2015).
source_text: The first part of Table 2 presents the results for sentence-level multimodal QE with BiRNN. The best model is BiRNN+Vis-embed-mult2, achieving a Pearson's r of 0.535, significantly outperforming the baseline (p-value<0.01). Visual features can, therefore, help to improve the performance of sentence-level neural-based QE systems significantly. Figure 2 presents the result of Williams significance test for BiRNN model variants. It is a correlation matrix that can be read as follows: the value in cell (i, j) is the p-value of Williams test for the change in performance of the model at row i compared to the model at column j (Graham, 2015) .

text: A related but distinct research area, readability, examines the reading difficulty of a text body. This area has explored readability effects across a wide range of contexts, including online versus paper media (Kurniawan and Zaphiris, 2001), color and contrast (Legge et al., 1990), and writing style (Bostian, 1983). The most widely known readability metric for English, Flesch-Kincaid (Kincaid et al., 1975), determines readability using counts of syllables and words. Other researchers have sought to refine this straightforward metric, demonstrating successful reading-level classification with unigram language models (Si and Callan, 2001) or SVM models constructed from these basic textual features (Pitler and Nenkova, 2008). Since earlier metrics appear to be adequate, recent work has concentrated on evaluating and comparing a variety of metrics across different domains (Sugawara et al., 2017; Redmiles et al., 2019). We draw on this readability literature to inform our feature selection, as readability seems to be intrinsically tied to reading time. Accordingly, we use the py-readability-metrics package to incorporate seven state-of-the-art metrics, which we add to our data for the modeling task (Section 4, Appendix B).
source_text: A separate but similar line of research, readability, measures the reading difficulty of a body of text. This research area has investigated effects of readability in a plethora of areas: online vs paper (Kurniawan and Zaphiris, 2001), color and contrast (Legge et al., 1990) , and writing style (Bostian, 1983) . The most famous readability metric for English, the Flesch-Kincaid (Kincaid et al., 1975) , uses the number of syllables and words to determine readability. Other scientists have attempted to improve upon this simple metric, showing success in reading level classification with unigram language models (Si and Callan, 2001) or SVM models built on top of these basic textual characteristics (Pitler and Nenkova, 2008) . As previous metrics seem to be sufficient, recent research has focused on evaluating and comparing the diverse metrics on different domains (Sugawara et al., 2017; Redmiles et al., 2019) . We use these readability works to influence our choice of features, as readability seems inherently interwoven with reading time. We employ the py-readability-metrics package to include 7 state-of-the-art metrics that we add to our data for the modeling task (Section 4, Appendix B).

text: Most extractive summarization models cast the task as a classification problem. In line with a recent method, SUMMARUNNER (Nallapati et al. 2017), we employ a neural-network-based encoder to construct representations of scenes and then apply a binary classifier to these representations to predict whether they should be included in the summary. For each scene \(s_i \in D\), we predict a label \(y_i \in \{0,1\}\) (where 1 indicates that \(s_i\) should be in the summary) and compute a score \(p(y_i \mid s_i, D, \theta)\) that measures \(s_i\)'s relevance to the summary, where \(\theta\) denotes the model parameters. We form a summary by selecting \(M\) sentences with the highest \(p(1 \mid s_i, D, \theta)\) values.
source_text: Most extractive models frame summarization as a classification problem. Following a recent approach (SUMMARUNNER; Nallapati et al. 2017), we use a neural network-based encoder to build representations for scenes and apply a binary classifier over these to predict whether they should be in the summary. For each scene s i ∈ D, we predict a label y i ∈ {0, 1} (where 1 means that s i must be in the summary) and assign a score p(y i |s i , D,θ) quantifying s i 's relevance to the summary (θ denotes model parameters). We assemble a summary by selecting M sentences with the top p(1|s i , D,θ).

text: Deep learning systems now routinely achieve very high performance across a wide range of natural language tasks, including machine translation (Wu et al., 2016; Vaswani et al., 2017), question answering (Henaff et al., 2016), visual question answering (Hudson and Manning, 2018), and natural language inference (Devlin et al., 2018; Storks et al., 2019). More recently, however, researchers have begun to ask whether such systems generalize in a systematic way (see §4).
source_text: Deep learning systems now regularly exhibit very high performance on a large variety of natural language tasks, including machine translation (Wu et al., 2016; Vaswani et al., 2017 ), question answering Henaff et al., 2016) , visual question answering (Hudson and Manning, 2018) , and natural language inference (Devlin et al., 2018; Storks et al., 2019) . Recently, however, researchers have asked whether such systems generalize systematically (see §4).

text: Analyzing trained models, explanation methods include occlusion- or gradient-based approaches that assess the relevance of input features for the output (Zintgraf et al., 2017; Harbecke et al., 2018), as well as probing tasks (Conneau et al., 2018; Kim et al., 2019) that examine whether particular features, for example in intermediate layers, are present. Closest to our approach is the rewriting of instances (Jia and Liang, 2017; Ribeiro et al., 2018); however, rather than using rewriting to evaluate model robustness, we employ it to test explicit error hypotheses, similarly to Wu et al. (2019).
source_text: Analyzing trained Models Explanation methods include occlusion or gradient-based methods, measuring the relevance of input features to the output (Zintgraf et al., 2017; Harbecke et al., 2018) , and probing tasks (Conneau et al., 2018; Kim et al., 2019) that probe the presence of specific features e.g. in intermediate layers. More similar to our approach is rewriting of instances (Jia and Liang, 2017; Ribeiro et al., 2018) but instead of evaluating model robustness we use rewriting to test explicit error hypotheses, similar to Wu et al. (2019) .

text: 2019), for example GQA (Hudson and Manning, 2019). In these tasks, the semantic scope required is generally restricted to individual sentences rather than to a collection of sentences; nearly all of the tasks are in English; and the data is drawn from running text and is mostly close-domain. Moreover, some of them depend on external knowledge bases or on high-level knowledge of physical models or experiments, as in ARC as classified by Boratko et al. (2018), which allows errors to accumulate from external components and makes it harder to analyze individual components such as reasoning. A further strand of early work in symbolic AI offers a different toolkit for modeling reasoning, including rule engines, rule-induction algorithms, logic programs, and case-based reasoning models (Kolodner, 1992). However, it is not straightforward to represent and model our task within these frameworks, because they usually require the specification of primitives, expressions, discrete features, and cases. In addition, the superiority of statistical and neural models over rule-based models has been demonstrated repeatedly. Our aim is to encourage researchers to integrate reasoning into statistical models rather than to replace them with symbolic models.
source_text: 2019) e.g., GQA (Hudson and Manning, 2019) . In these tasks, the required level of semantics is mostly limited to single sentences rather than a collection; almost all tasks target English; data is derived from running text and is mostly close-domain. In addition, some require external knowledge bases or high-level knowledge on physical models or experiments as in ARC classified by Boratko et al. (2018) , which leaves room for accumulating errors from external parts and complicates the analysis of individual parts like reasoning. Another body of early work on symbolic AI provides a different set of tools to model reasoning such as rule-engines, rule-induction algorithms, logic programs and case-based reasoning models (Kolodner, 1992) . However, it is not trivial to represent and model our task in these frameworks, since they mostly require defining primitives, expressions, discrete features and cases. Furthermore, the strength of statistical/neural models has been repeatedly shown to surpass rule-based models. Our goal is to encourage researchers to incorporate reasoning into statistical models, rather than replacing them with symbolic models.

text: Impact of Att-GGNN. When a standard GGNN (A3, -w/o Multi-Relation & Attention) is used to encode the semantic graph, performance declines to 14.15 (−3.61%) in BLEU-4 relative to the model equipped with Att-GGNN (A4, -w/o Multi-Task). This indicates that distinct entity types and their semantic relations supply auxiliary information that is necessary for generating meaningful questions. Our Att-GGNN model (P2) introduces attention into the standard GGNN and thus effectively exploits information across multiple node and edge types.
source_text: • Impact of Att-GGNN. Using a normal GGNN (A3, -w/o Multi-Relation & Attention) to encode the semantic graph, performance drops to 14.15 (−3.61%) in BLEU-4 compared to the model with Att-GGNN (A4, -w/o Multi-Task). This reveals that different entity types and their semantic relations provide auxiliary information needed to generate meaningful questions. Our Att-GGNN model (P2) incorporates attention into the normal GGNN, effectively leverages the information across multiple node and edge types.

text: Before any analyses were conducted, the Switchboard and Buckeye corpora were each randomly split into equally sized Training and Test sets. The Training sets were then used for exploratory statistical analyses and for selecting the values of several model hyperparameters. After this stage, all parameters and statistical analyses were fixed and preregistered with the Open Science Foundation. 8 We fit multiple linear regressions to assess the effect of confusability on word duration. Throughout, contextual confusability is defined according to Equation 9. Word durations are log-transformed. The following covariates are standard in the literature and are therefore included in our analyses: speaker identity; part of speech; unigram prior surprisal; speech rate (the average speech rate, in syllables per second, of the utterance containing the target word); and word length (measured in segments and syllables). Several additional covariates are less straightforward and are discussed in greater detail below: segmental inventory factors; forward and backward surprisal; neighborhood size and log weighted neighborhood density; and unigram confusability.
source_text: Prior to any analyses, the Switchboard and Buckeye corpora were each randomly divided into evenly-sized Training and Test sets. The Training sets were used for exploratory statistical analyses, and for determining the values of several model hyperparameters. Following this, all parameters and statistical analyses were frozen, and preregistered with the Open Science Foundation. 8 We perform several linear regressions in order to determine the effect of confusability on word duration. Contextual confusability is defined throughout using Equation 9. Word durations are logtransformed. The following covariates are standard in the literature, and are included in our analyses: speaker identity; part of speech; unigram prior surprisal; speech rate (the average rate of speech, in syllables per second, of the utterance containing the target word); word length (measured by number of segments and syllables). Several covariates that are included are more non-trivial, and are discussed in more detail below: segmental inventory factors; forward and backward surprisal; neighborhood size and log weighted neighborhood density; and unigram confusability.

text: An additional quality indicator is the number of erroneous triples introduced by extraction mistakes in OPIEC, for example, (“Finland”, “is the western part of”, “the balkan peninsula”), (“William Macaskill”, “is vice-president of”, “giving”), or errors arising from alternative mentions. We also examined the evaluation data for generic mentions. Such mentions primarily encode conceptual knowledge, as in (“computer science”, “had backgrounds in”, “mathematics”). Other generic triples, such as (“Patrick E.”, “joined the team in”, “the season”), also convey conceptual content, but lack sufficient context to disambiguate “the season”.
source_text: An additional quality assessment is the number of wrong triples caused by extraction errors in OPIEC, e.g., ("Finland", "is the western part of", "the balkan peninsula"), ("William Macaskill", "is vice-president of", "giving"), or errors in alternative mentions. We also looked for generic mentions in the evaluation data. Such mentions contain mostly conceptual knowledge like in ("computer science", "had backgrounds in", "mathematics"). Other generic triples, like ("Patrick E.", "joined the team in", "the season"), have conceptual meaning, but miss context to disambiguate "the season".

text: In this paper, we propose an effective framework for fine-tuning BERT and XLNet on multimodal input. The framework preserves the core architectures of BERT and XL-Net unchanged, while adding only a carefully designed Multimodal Adaptation Gate (MAG) to these models. Through an attention mechanism conditioned on nonverbal behaviors, MAG effectively projects informative visual and acoustic cues into a vector characterized by both trajectory and magnitude. During fine-tuning, this adaptation vector updates the internal state of BERT and XLNet, enabling the models to adapt smoothly to multimodal input. In our experiments, we employ the CMU-MOSI (Zadeh et al., 2016) and CMU-MOSEI (Zadeh et al., 2018d) multimodal language datasets, with particular emphasis on the central NLP task of multimodal sentiment analysis. We evaluate the performance of MAG-BERT and MAG-XLNet against the above (a) and (b) settings in both classification and regression-based sentiment analysis. Our results show that fine-tuning these advanced pre-trained Transformers with MAG produces consistent gains, despite the fact that BERT and XLNet were never trained on multimodal data.
source_text: In this paper, we present a successful framework for fine-tuning BERT and XLNet for multimodal input. Our framework allows the BERT and XL-Net core structures to remain intact, and only attaches a carefully designed Multimodal Adaptation Gate (MAG) to the models. Using an attention conditioned on the nonverbal behaviors, MAG essentially maps the informative visual and acoustic factors to a vector with a trajectory and magnitude. During fine-tuning, this adaptation vector modifies the internal state of the BERT and XLNet, allowing the models to seamlessly adapt to the multimodal input. In our experiments we use the CMU-MOSI (Zadeh et al., 2016) and CMU-MOSEI (Zadeh et al., 2018d) datasets of multimodal language, with a specific focus on the core NLP task of multimodal sentiment analysis. We compare the performance of MAG-BERT and MAG-XLNet to the above (a) and (b) scenarios in both classification and regression sentiment analysis. Our findings demonstrate that fine-tuning these advanced pre-trained Transformers using MAG yields consistent improvement, even though BERT and XLNet were never trained on multimodal data.

text: Snorkel model The Snorkel framework does not natively support sequence labelling tasks, since its data points are assumed to be independent. Nevertheless, heuristics may be employed to identify named-entity candidates and then apply labelling functions to determine their most probable labels. For this baseline, we use the three functions nnp detector, proper detector, and compound detector (see Appendix A) to produce candidate spans. We then construct a matrix that records the output of each labelling function for every span, including a special “abstain” value to indicate the absence of a prediction, and apply the matrix-completion-style method of Ratner et al. (2019) to combine the predictions from all functions.
source_text: Snorkel model The Snorkel framework does not directly support sequence labelling tasks as data points are required to be independent. However, heuristics can be used to extract named-entity candidates and then apply labelling functions to infer their most likely labels . For this baseline, we use the three functions nnp detector, proper detector and compound detector (see Appendix A) to generate candidate spans. We then create a matrix expressing the output of each labelling function for each span (including a specific "abstain" value to denote the absence of prediction) and run the matrix-completionstyle approach of Ratner et al. (2019) to aggregate the predictions from all functions.

text: Unsupervised domain adaptation: Unsupervised domain adaptation seeks to transfer knowledge from a source domain so as to make predictions on new instances in a target domain, which often exhibits substantially different properties. Earlier methods frequently attempt to adapt the feature space by using pivots (Blitzer et al., 2006; Blitzer et al., 2007; Ziser and Reichart, 2017) in order to construct domain-invariant representations of predictive features. Other approaches learn low-dimensional transformed features of the data (Guo et al., 2009; Glorot et al., 2011; Chen et al., 2012; Yu and Jiang, 2016; Barnes et al., 2018). Finally, some methods partition the feature space into general and domain-specific features (Daumé III, 2007). Multi-task learning can likewise enhance cross-domain performance (Peng and Dredze, 2017). More recently, Han and Eisenstein (2019) introduced domain-adaptive fine-tuning, in which contextualised embeddings are first fine-tuned on both the source and target domains using a language-modelling objective and then fine-tuned on labelled source-domain data. This method outperforms several strong baselines trained on the target domain for the WNUT 2016 NER task (Strauss et al., 2016).
source_text: Unsupervised domain adaptation: Unsupervised domain adaptation attempts to adapt knowledge from a source domain to predict new instances in a target domain which often has substantially different characteristics. Earlier approaches often try to adapt the feature space using pivots (Blitzer et al., 2006 (Blitzer et al., , 2007 Ziser and Reichart, 2017) to create domain-invariant representations of predictive features. Others learn low-dimensional transformation features of the data (Guo et al., 2009; Glorot et al., 2011; Chen et al., 2012; Yu and Jiang, 2016; Barnes et al., 2018) . Finally, some approaches divide the feature space into general and domain-dependent features (Daumé III, 2007) . Multi-task learning can also improve cross-domain performance (Peng and Dredze, 2017) . Recently, Han and Eisenstein (2019) proposed domain-adaptive fine-tuning, where contextualised embeddings are first fine-tuned to both the source and target domains with a language modelling loss and subsequently fine-tuned to source domain labelled data. This approach outperforms several strong baselines trained on the target domain of the WNUT 2016 NER task (Strauss et al., 2016) .

text: We use dictionaries from MUSE (Conneau et al., 2018), a widely used BLI benchmark, with the standard split: training on 5K source-word translations and testing on 1.5K words for BLI. For each language, we train three projection-based CLWE, including canonical correlation analysis (Faruqui and Dyer, 2014). Figure 3 reports, for each CLWE, accuracy on document classification (left) and unlabeled attachment score (UAS) on dependency parsing (right). Relative to the original embeddings (gray), retrofitting with the training dictionary (pink) improves average downstream task scores, confirming that fully exploiting the training dictionary benefits downstream tasks. Adding a synthetic dictionary (orange) further improves test accuracy in some languages.
